title: Preparation of Parts and Adequate Lighting
narrationScript: Penetrant testing finds a flaw only when penetrant can get into it. That single fact drives everything in this lesson. Before any penetrant touches the part, two conditions have to be true. The surface must be clean and dry, with every flaw open to the surface, and the lighting at the processing and inspection stations must meet your procedure. Get either one wrong and the rest of the process is theater. Start with preparation. Capillary action can only pull penetrant into an opening that is actually open. Anything sitting in the flaw, or sitting over the flaw, defeats the test before it starts. Think of contamination in three families. The first family is soils. Oil, grease, cutting fluid, and general shop grime either fill the flaw or coat the surface so the penetrant cannot wet it. The second family is solid barriers. Paint, plating, heavy rust, and scale bridge over an opening and seal it, which is why most procedures require coatings to be stripped from the area of interest before testing. The third family is the sneakiest, mechanical smear. Operations like shot peening, abrasive blasting, wire brushing, grinding, and some machining drag soft surface metal sideways and fold it over the mouth of a crack like a lid. The part looks beautifully clean, and the crack underneath is sealed. That is why many specifications require an etch after smearing operations, to dissolve the thin smeared layer and reopen the flaws, or they simply forbid those operations before penetrant testing. Choose the cleaning method to match the contaminant. Solvent wiping and spraying handle local oils and films. Vapor degreasing does an excellent job on oils and greases and leaves flaws dry. Alkaline cleaning handles heavy soils on batches of parts, and ultrasonic cleaning adds scrubbing action that reaches into tight openings. Rust and scale usually call for acid pickling or etching. Two warnings come with chemical cleaning. First, rinse it off completely. Acid and chromate residues left in a flaw can kill the glow of fluorescent penetrant, so an indication never forms even though the penetrant got in. Second, dry the part after any aqueous cleaning. Water trapped in a flaw blocks penetrant the same way oil does. Typical procedures dry parts in warm air or a low oven, with the part temperature commonly held at or below about one hundred sixty degrees Fahrenheit. Before penetrant goes on, the part should be clean, dry, and inside the standard temperature band, commonly forty to one hundred twenty-five degrees Fahrenheit. Now lighting, because you process and inspect with your eyes. For visible red dye penetrant, the requirement is white light, commonly at least one hundred footcandles at the surface you are examining, which is roughly one thousand lux. Measure it with a calibrated light meter at the part, not at the lamp, and record the reading. For fluorescent penetrant, the rules flip. You need strong ultraviolet light and almost no white light. Typical procedure values call for UV-A intensity of at least one thousand microwatts per square centimeter at the part surface, with ambient white light held to about two footcandles or less in the inspection booth. Check the lamp with a UV meter on a regular schedule, because output falls as lamps and filters age. Give your eyes several minutes to dark adapt before you start evaluating, and keep photochromic or heavily tinted lenses out of the booth, because they interfere with seeing the glow. Remember the wash station too. On a fluorescent water-washable line you judge the wash endpoint by watching background glow fade under UV light, so the wash station needs its own UV lamp that meets your procedure. One more habit separates professionals from button pushers. Verify lighting before you process, not after. A batch processed and inspected under weak UV is a batch you cannot trust, and the only honest fix is to restore the light and look again. Clean surface, open flaws, dry part, verified light. That is the foundation the whole penetrant process stands on.
imagePrompt: A gloved technician wipes a machined steel aerospace fitting with a lint-free cloth at a brightly lit preparation bench, solvent spray cans and a handheld light meter resting beside the part.

sections.heading: No Entry, No Indication
sections.body:
Penetrant testing detects only discontinuities that are **open to the surface**, and only when penetrant can physically reach them. Capillary action is powerful, but it cannot pull liquid through oil, water, paint, or a fold of smeared metal. Surface preparation is therefore not housekeeping - it is the first process-control step of the test.

Three categories of contamination defeat penetrant entry:

- **Soils** - oil, grease, cutting and machining fluids, carbon, and shop grime. They fill the flaw cavity or coat the surface so penetrant cannot wet it.
- **Solid barriers** - paint, plating, heavy rust, and scale. These bridge the flaw opening and seal it; coatings are normally stripped from the area of interest before testing.
- **Mechanical smear** - peening, abrasive blasting, wire brushing, grinding, and some machining drag ductile surface metal across flaw mouths and close them.

The part must also be **dry** and within the standard processing temperature band, commonly **40-125 °F (4-52 °C)**, before penetrant is applied. A flaw still wet with rinse water or cleaning solvent is a flaw that penetrant cannot enter.

sections.caption: Four conditions at the mouth of a crack: clean and open, filled with soil, sealed by smeared metal, and bridged by a coating - only the first admits penetrant.

sections.heading: Matching the Cleaning Method to the Contaminant
sections.body:
Pick the cleaning method for the contaminant actually present, not for convenience:

- **Solvent wipe or spray** - local oils, greases, and films; the field standard before portable PT.
- **Vapor degreasing** - excellent bulk removal of oils and greases; parts emerge dry and slightly warm.
- **Alkaline cleaning** - heavy soils on batched parts; must be followed by a thorough rinse and complete drying.
- **Ultrasonic cleaning** - adds cavitation scrubbing that reaches into tight openings on small parts.
- **Steam or detergent cleaning** - large surfaces and structural work.
- **Acid pickling or etching** - rust and scale removal, and re-opening flaws after metal-smearing operations.

Two chemical-cleaning rules protect the test itself. **Rinse residues away completely:** acid and chromate residues trapped in a flaw can quench fluorescence, so a fluorescent indication never forms even though penetrant entered. And **dry thoroughly after any aqueous step** - warm air or a low-temperature oven, with part temperature commonly held at or below about **160 °F**, framed as a typical procedure value. Cleaners that merely look dry on the surface can still hold liquid down inside a tight crack, so allow real drying time.

sections.title: Smeared metal is invisible sabotage
sections.body: Shot peening, abrasive blasting, wire brushing, grinding, and aggressive machining can fold a thin layer of metal over crack mouths. The part looks **cleaner than ever** - and the cracks are sealed shut. When such operations precede PT, many specifications require a chemical **etch** to dissolve the smeared layer and re-open the flaws, or they require PT to be performed **before** the smearing operation. Your written procedure governs.

sections.heading: Dry Before You Apply
sections.body:
Drying after cleaning deserves its own attention because it fails quietly. A part can feel dry to a gloved hand while rinse water, detergent solution, or solvent still sits in the very flaws you are trying to find. Liquid in the flaw blocks penetrant entry outright, or dilutes the penetrant that does enter until the indication is too weak to see.

Typical practice allows solvents a flash-off period at room temperature and dries water-cleaned parts with warm circulating air or a drying oven, keeping part temperature at or below about **160 °F** so penetrant constituents and part condition are not affected. After oven drying, let the part cool back into the standard application band - commonly **40-125 °F** - before penetrant goes on. Applying penetrant to an overheated part dries the penetrant film and ruins the next stage before it begins. Treat **clean, dry, in-band** as a three-part gate: all three must be true before the first drop of penetrant is applied.

sections.heading: Adequate Lighting: Two Regimes
sections.body:
Lighting is a process requirement, not a comfort item, and it is verified **before** processing and inspection - with a calibrated meter, at the working surface, with the reading recorded.

**Visible (Type II) penetrant** depends on color contrast: red dye against a white developer background. Practices such as ASTM E165/E1417 and ASME Section V, Article 6 call for adequate white light at the examination surface, commonly a minimum of about **100 footcandles (≈ 1,000 lux)**. Light falls off rapidly with distance, so a fixture that looks bright across the shop may be far too weak at the part.

**Fluorescent (Type I) penetrant** depends on brightness contrast: a glowing indication against a dark field. Typical procedure values require **UV-A intensity of at least 1,000 µW/cm²** at the part surface and **ambient white light of about 2 fc (≈ 20 lux) or less** in the darkened booth. Allow your eyes **several minutes of dark adaptation** before evaluating, and keep photochromic or heavily tinted lenses out of the booth - they interfere with seeing dim indications. UV lamps and filters age, so intensity is checked periodically with a UV-A meter, and fluorescent wash stations need UV light as well, because the fading background glow is your wash-endpoint gauge.

sections.title: UV-A discipline
sections.body: Never look directly into a UV-A lamp, and take a cracked or missing filter out of service immediately - the filter is what keeps hazardous shorter wavelengths out of the beam. Cover prolonged skin exposure under high-intensity sources, and remember that white garments and paper fluoresce: keep them out of the inspection field so they do not create glare or false background.

sections.caption: Lighting regimes for visible and fluorescent PT: white light verified at the surface for Type II, and a darkened booth with measured UV-A intensity for Type I.
Lighting requirements at a glance - typical procedure values; your written procedure governs.
rows: Even, glare-free illumination of the test area
Calibrated white-light meter at the surface
Ambient white ≤ 2 fc; several minutes dark adaptation; no photochromic/tinted lenses
Wash endpoint judged by fading background glow

figures.title: Adequate lighting for visible and fluorescent PT

checkQuestions.question: A casting was abrasive blasted to strip scale just before penetrant testing, and the surface now looks bright and uniformly clean. What is the process-control concern?
options: Blast media residue will fluoresce and cause false indications
Blasting smears ductile surface metal over crack mouths, so real flaws may be sealed and missed
The roughened surface will make the penetrant dwell too long
There is no concern - blasting is the preferred preparation for PT
checkQuestions.explanation: Abrasive blasting (like peening, wire brushing, and grinding) folds smeared metal over flaw openings. The part looks clean, but penetrant cannot enter; an etch is commonly required to re-open the flaws.

checkQuestions.question: Why must acid or chromate cleaning residues be completely rinsed from a part before fluorescent penetrant testing?
options: They make the surface too slippery for the developer to adhere
They raise the part temperature above the standard band
Residues trapped in flaws can quench fluorescence, so an indication never forms even if penetrant enters
They turn the penetrant from Type I into Type II
checkQuestions.explanation: Acid and chromate residues left in a flaw can kill the fluorescent response of the penetrant that enters it - the flaw is filled but produces no visible glow under UV-A.

checkQuestions.question: For visible (Type II) penetrant evaluation, a typical procedure requires white light of about:
options: 2 fc at the lamp face
100 fc (≈ 1,000 lux) measured at the examination surface with a calibrated meter
1,000 µW/cm² measured anywhere in the room
10 fc estimated by eye at the part
checkQuestions.explanation: Visible PT relies on color contrast and typically requires at least about 100 footcandles (roughly 1,000 lux) of white light verified with a calibrated meter at the surface being examined.

checkQuestions.question: Which set of conditions describes a properly prepared fluorescent inspection booth?
options: UV-A ≥ 1,000 µW/cm² at the surface, ambient white light ≤ 2 fc, eyes dark-adapted for several minutes
UV-A ≥ 100 µW/cm² at the lamp, ambient white light ≥ 100 fc
Any UV level, provided the inspector wears photochromic lenses
White light of 1,000 lux at the surface with the UV lamp off
checkQuestions.explanation: Typical procedure values are at least 1,000 µW/cm² of UV-A at the part surface with ambient white light held to about 2 fc, plus several minutes of dark adaptation - and photochromic or tinted lenses interfere and are kept out.

checkQuestions.question: A batch of parts comes out of a hot alkaline wash followed by a water rinse. Before penetrant can be applied, the parts must be:
options: Wire brushed to restore surface brightness
Quenched in cold water so they are easier to handle
Sprayed immediately while still wet so the penetrant mixes with the rinse water
Thoroughly dried and allowed to return to the standard temperature band, commonly 40-125 °F
checkQuestions.explanation: Water trapped in flaws blocks or dilutes penetrant, and an overheated part dries the penetrant film. The gate before application is clean, dry, and in-band.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §2.1 (preparation of parts)
ASTM E165 / ASTM E1417 (penetrant examination - general practice)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6 (liquid penetrant examination - general practice)
